lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — DAR — the drug-antibody ratio
Welcome back.
Let's pick up with a number — a single number that, more than almost any other parameter, controls the behavior of an ADC.
It's called the drug-antibody ratio, or DAR, and it's exactly what it sounds like: the average number of payload molecules conjugated to each antibody.
DAR is the warhead count per missile, and choosing the right DAR is one of the central engineering trade-offs in ADC design.
The intuition is straightforward.
More warheads per missile means more killing power delivered per molecule of antibody.
If you can hang ten payloads off an antibody instead of two, in principle you're delivering five times the cell-killing dose per antibody-cell binding event.
That sounds great.
Why not just keep cranking up the DAR — twenty, thirty, fifty payloads per antibody?
The reason is that the antibody is not an infinitely modifiable scaffold.

When you attach payload molecules to an antibody, you're typically attaching them to specific amino acid residues — most commonly lysines, which are scattered all over the surface of the protein, or cysteines, which are typically generated by partially reducing the antibody's internal disulfide bonds.
Each conjugation site changes the local chemistry.
Each payload molecule is typically pretty hydrophobic.
As you add more and more hydrophobic payloads to the protein surface, the antibody starts to look less like a soluble protein and more like a greasy aggregation-prone mess.
Here's what actually happens, empirically.
At low DARs — two to four — the antibody behaves more or less normally in plasma.
Long half-life, good tumor penetration, manageable toxicity.
At intermediate DARs — four to six — you start to see some effects on pharmacokinetics, but the drug is still well-behaved.
At high DARs — eight and above — historically, you saw rapid clearance from circulation, aggregation in solution, and a narrower therapeutic window.

The high-DAR molecules were getting cleared by the liver — particularly by Kupffer cells, the resident macrophages of the liver, which recognize the hydrophobic patches on the protein surface as something to eat.
So for the first generation of ADCs — including Mylotarg and T-DM1 and even Adcetris — the consensus DAR was in the 3-to-4 range.
Anything higher seemed to compromise the drug.
T-DXd broke this rule.
T-DXd has an average DAR of 8 — twice the conventional limit — and it works.
How?
Because the Daiichi Sankyo team engineered the linker and payload to be much more hydrophilic than previous designs.
The DXd payload itself has hydrophilic functional groups, and the tetrapeptide linker carries a polyethylene glycol-like spacer that adds more water-loving character.
The net result is that even with eight payloads per antibody, the molecule stays soluble, doesn't aggregate, and isn't cleared faster than normal by the liver.
The therapeutic window is preserved.
This was a significant engineering achievement, and it has changed how the field thinks about DAR.
The old rule — keep DAR low — has been replaced by a new rule: optimize linker and payload hydrophilicity to enable higher DARs without pharmacokinetic compromise.
Several new ADCs in development are targeting DARs of 8 or even higher, betting that with the right chemistry, the increased potency per antibody outweighs the risks.
There's one more wrinkle.
When you do conjugation chemistry to attach payloads to an antibody, you don't get a clean molecule with exactly the same number of payloads on every antibody.
You get a distribution.
Some antibodies will have zero payloads.
Some will have two.
Some will have eight.
The DAR you report is the average across the population.
The actual distribution might look like a curve — a bell curve, or sometimes a bimodal distribution with peaks at zero and four.
This heterogeneity matters clinically.
The antibodies with zero payloads are basically dead weight — they bind to the target, occupy receptors, but deliver no drug.
They compete with the loaded antibodies for tumor binding, diluting out the effective dose.
The antibodies with very high payload loading are more toxic and get cleared faster.
The intermediate species — somewhere around the average DAR — are the workhorses.
For Mylotarg, this was a particular problem.

The original conjugation chemistry produced a population with a substantial fraction of unconjugated antibody, and that unconjugated antibody was eating up CD33 binding sites without delivering payload.
When researchers analyzed why Mylotarg sometimes worked and sometimes didn't, the heterogeneity of the DAR distribution was a significant contributor.

This is one of the reasons site-specific conjugation has become a major area of ADC research — because if you can put payloads at exactly defined positions on the antibody, you get a much more uniform DAR distribution, often referred to as a "homogeneous" ADC.
Same number of payloads on every antibody.
No dead-weight unconjugated species.
No over-loaded toxic species.
Just one defined molecule.
That's the next segment.
For now, take away: DAR is the warhead count.
Choose it carefully, balance it against pharmacokinetics, and remember that the average hides a distribution.
The field has moved from a default of DAR 3-4 to DAR 8 for the most modern ADCs, enabled by better linker chemistry.
And the next frontier is making sure every antibody in the batch has exactly the same DAR.
